title: Multi-Qubit Entanglement of Unit Cell Pairs in SiMOS
authors: Cameron Jones, Jonathan Y. Huang, Santiago Serrano, MengKe Feng, Gerardo A. Paz-Silva, Tuomo Tanttu, Paul Steinacker, Fay E. Hudson, Wee Han Lim, Nikolay V. Abrosimov, Hans-Joachim Pohl, Michael L. W. Thewalt, Andrew S. Dzurak, Andre Saraiva, Arne Laucht, Chih Hwan Yang
abstract: Spin qubits in silicon-MOS (SiMOS) quantum dots have recently demonstrated compatibility with existing industry standard CMOS fabrication techniques. These devices have routinely achieved single- and two-qubit gate fidelities above 99% and demonstrated highly entangled two-qubit Bell states in isolated double quantum dot (DQD) unit cells, however coupling between unit cells has remained challenging. In this work, we present a two unit cell, four-qubit SiMOS processor with universal controllability and fully parallelised state initialisation and readout. We use this processor to generate maximally entangled three-qubit states, including the Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger (GHZ) state, and certify multipartite entanglement through violation of the classical Mermin-witness bound. By using a fully symmetric dynamically decoupled gate sequence to create our entangled states, we are able to preserve the lifetime of the entanglement beyond $T_2^*$, to a time limited instead by $T_2^\textrm{Hahn}$. These demonstrations pave a road to the scalable operation of larger SiMOS processors, and achieving high purity, long-lived multi-qubit entangled states in them.
summary: This paper demonstrates a four-qubit SiMOS quantum processor that successfully generates and verifies three-qubit GHZ and cluster states. It achieves this by implementing parallelized readout and a specialized gate sequence that protects entanglement from dephasing. This work provides a significant step toward scaling silicon-based quantum computing using industry-standard CMOS techniques.
main_problem: Scaling SiMOS quantum dot processors beyond isolated double quantum dot unit cells by achieving high-fidelity coupling between unit cells and implementing efficient, parallelized initialization and readout.
main_result: Demonstrated a four-qubit SiMOS processor capable of generating maximally entangled three-qubit GHZ and cluster states, with entanglement lifetimes extended toward the Hahn-echo limit using a laddered decoupling sequence.
method: The researchers used a decoupled-CZ (dCZ) gate set, FPGA-based heralded initialization, and parallelized Pauli spin blockade readout, while employing Gate Set Tomography and Mermin-witness operators for state verification.
model_or_system: A four-qubit SiMOS (Silicon Metal-Oxide Semiconductor) processor consisting of a linear array of quantum dots in two coupled double quantum dot (DQD) unit cells, utilizing isotopically enriched silicon.
key_observables: Single- and two-qubit gate fidelities, Mermin witness values, coherence times (T2* and THahn), Rabi frequencies, and readout fidelity.
important_parameters: Larmor frequencies ~11.4 GHz, T2* between 4.8-6.2 us, THahn between 76.3-87.2 us, and two-qubit dCZ fidelities ~95.5-95.8%.
main_assumptions: The dephasing is primarily driven by charge noise rather than hyperfine coupling; SPAM errors can be modeled and corrected via a uniform depolarizing channel.
figures_summary: Figures demonstrate the comparison of sequential vs. simultaneous readout, the calibration of detuning for parallel operation, the implementation of the laddered gate sequence, and the decay of Mermin values as a function of idle and exchange time.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the SiMOS platform, describes the architecture and control/readout hardware, details the gate implementation and decoupling strategies, presents the generation and verification of multi-qubit entanglement, and concludes with a discussion on scalability and noise sources.
why_interesting: The use of a laddered decoupling sequence to extend entanglement lifetime toward the Hahn-echo limit and the demonstration of multipartite entanglement in a scalable semiconductor architecture are highly relevant to open quantum systems and quantum network research.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1 | Device overview and controllability. a, A false coloured scanning electron microscope image of a device similar to that used in this work. Green (barrier gates) are metallization layer 1, red (plunger gates) are layer 2, purple (J gates) are layer 3, and blue (ESR antenna) are layer 4. b, Schematic cross-section of the device in the dotted region of (a). Electrons are accumulated at the Si/SiOx interface under the plunger (P) gates. The device is operated in the (3,5,5,3) charge configuration. c, Single-qubit Rabi oscillations of the qubits Q1-Q4 corresponding to qubits under gates P1-P4 as a function of applied microwave pulse duration. d, Decoupled controlled phase oscillations...

Fig. 2 | Device operating sequence and readout techniques. a, Schematic diagram showing the sequence structure for a single-shot experiment using the sequential readout technique. The four-qubit heralded initialisation uses a real-time logic state filter that re-initialises the processor unless blockaded parity is measured in both DQDs. The order of both the initialisation and terminating readouts are interchanged each measurement shot. The total measurement time is displayed below, assuming a 10 µs control operation Uc. b, Two-dimensional readout histogram from GST experiments using sequential readout method. State classification thresholds are displayed as dotted black lines. See Extended...

Fig. 3 | Three-qubit entangled state measurements. a, Circuit used to generate a three-qubit cluster state (equivalent to the GHZ state up to local single-qubit gates) and perform state tomography. {P1Q} are the set of single-qubit pre-measurement rotations required to perform state tomography. Two-qubit operations {P2Q} are also used on Q3 and Q4 to obtain ZI/IZ measurements (see Extended Data Fig. 6b for circuit). b, Reconstructed density matrix. A local basis transformation has been applied in post-processing to represent it as the well-recognised GHZ state density matrix. The SPAM error associated with dephasing from the tomographic operations ({P1Q}/{P2Q}) is compensated for in both...

Fig. 4 | Entanglement lifetime. a, T Hahn 2 -style measurement on the three qubit state |+++⟩measured by removing the CZ operations from the circuit in Fig. 3a and adding idle times. b, Mermin value as function of idle time, representing the longevity of the entanglement between the three qubits. This is measured by inserting wait times on either side of the refocusing pulses. c, Measurement of the oscillations in Mermin value when the exchange time is extended. Here the exchange time for both J2 and J3 are increased proportionally with each other. Error bounds on T2 values represent 1σ uncertainty.
